By the time we opened, we had been working on it for years.
While the competitors were scaling fast, we built our team.
The turning point came in the second year.
A procedure the team had refined produced a breakthrough.
The success had been in the making for years.
So, before the clinic opened, how long had you actually been working on it?
Honestly? By the time we opened, I had been building the plan for the better part of a decade.
A decade. And in those early years, what was happening behind the scenes?
It was an uphill battle. While the bigger clinics were scaling fast, I was still hiring a team from the ground up.
So when did it change? What was the turning point?
The second year. A procedure we had been refining for months finally produced a breakthrough.
And the world called it an overnight success. Is that fair?
Against the odds, the clinic came into its own -- but overnight? No. It had been in the making for years.
That is the part people miss. They see the breakthrough, but never the groundwork you had been laying.
The turning point came after years of quiet groundwork.
Against the odds, it began to come into its own.
The success looked sudden, but it had been in the making for years.
What they called luck was really grit and years of work.

Welcome back to the show. Today's guest is a founder whose story almost did not happen. For years, long before anyone had heard her name, she had been building something quietly, from the ground up. When I first met her, the clinic was still an uphill battle. She had been working nights, she had been losing sleep, and the momentum she needed had not yet arrived. Then came the turning point. A procedure her team had been refining for months finally produced a breakthrough, and against the odds, the whole thing began to take off. Here is what I love about her story. By the time the world called it an overnight success, she had already been at it for the better part of a decade. The groundwork was the story. Let's hear it in her own words.

When people ask how the clinic started, I never begin at the launch. I begin earlier, in the years when I had been laying the groundwork and nothing had worked yet. I had been building the plan one partnership at a time, and honestly, it was an uphill battle. While the bigger clinics were scaling fast, I was still hiring my first team from the ground up. The turning point came in the second year. We had been refining one procedure for months when it finally produced a breakthrough, and the momentum I had been chasing arrived almost overnight. Against the odds, the clinic came into its own. Looking back, the success that seemed sudden had really been in the making for years. What they called luck, I call grit.
